📈 QQQ — 1-Hour Chart Explanation

Reference date: 2026-04-16  |  Timeframe: 1-hour bars  |  Generated: 2026-04-29 15:01

Jump to: 1. Data Overview | 2. Pivot Detection | 3. Regime Classification | 4. Channel Construction | 5. S/R Zones | 6. Volume Analysis | 7. Breakout (if applicable) | 8. Fan Lines (if applicable) | 9. Final Verdict

This document walks through the complete reasoning behind the 1-hour trendline chart for QQQ. Each section explains one step of the analysis, shows which data triggered each decision, and includes progressive charts so you can see exactly how each overlay is built on top of the raw price data.

1. Data Overview

This chart covers the last 60 trading days of 1-hour bars (approximately 420 bars). The analysis anchors all percentage thresholds to ATR(14) so they scale automatically with recent volatility.

  • Ticker: QQQ
  • Timeframe: 1-hour bars
  • Reference date: 2026-04-16
  • Bar count: 627 bars (1-hour intervals)
  • Date range: 2025-12-05 09:30 → 2026-04-16 15:00
  • Price range: $555.60 – $642.18 (15.6% span)
  • Latest close: $640.41
  • ATR(14): $3.422 per bar (0.53% of price) — used throughout as the volatility yardstick
  • Scale mode: ARITHMETIC — Arithmetic scale was used — price range is within 20% so linear spacing is appropriate.

The chart below shows the raw price bars before any analysis overlays are added.

2. Step 1: Pivot Detection

Pivots are the local turning points the engine uses as anchors for trendlines and support/resistance zones. The detection follows Grimes's three-order hierarchy — only first-order pivots (the most significant local extremes) are used here.

Detection Rules (1-hour tier parameters)

  • Window: ±4 bars — a high is a pivot only if it is strictly greater than all bars within 4 bars on each side (similarly for lows).
  • ATR confirmation: The price swing from the previous pivot of the opposite type must be ≥ 1.0× ATR(14) = at least $3.422. This filters micro-wiggles that are not meaningful swings.
  • Alternation: Pivots must strictly alternate high → low → high → … The zigzag is enforced so consecutive same-side pivots are impossible.
  • Minimum separation: Same-side pivots must be ≥ 10 bars apart to prevent clusters of nearby pivots being treated as distinct turning points.
  • Intervening swing: The counterswing between two same-side pivots must be ≥ 1.0× ATR(14) ≈ $3.422 — ensures "open water" between pivots, ruling out minor retracements.
  • Channel span: The full set of anchors must span ≥ 30 bars so the trendline is not based on a tiny, unrepresentative window.

Why some candidates are rejected
  • A local peak that has a neighbour within 4 bars which is equally high or higher → fails the strict inequality window test.
  • A peak where the preceding swing from the nearest low is smaller than $3.422 (the ATR threshold) → rejected as noise.
  • Two highs too close together (gap < 10 bars) → only the most extreme of the cluster is kept.

The chart below adds these pivot markers (▼ for highs in red, ▲ for lows in green) on top of the raw bars.

3. Step 2: Regime Classification

Regime classification determines the overall market condition. The engine evaluates in strict priority order: TREND → BREAK → OTHERS. A TREND is only declared if all four criteria below pass simultaneously.

Criterion 1: Dow Theory Pivot Sequence

An uptrend requires higher highs and higher lows; a downtrend requires lower lows and lower highs. A tolerance of 0.25% is applied — a pivot may be up to 0.25% below the previous same-side pivot and still count as "higher" (catches minor pullbacks without breaking the trend classification).

  • Higher Highs (HH) check:
    • High 1: $628.84 → High 2: $629.21 ↑ higher ✓
    • High 2: $629.21 → High 3: $625.52 ↓ lower ✗
    • High 3: $625.52 → High 4: $620.81 ↓ lower ✗
    • High 4: $620.81 → High 5: $627.94 ↑ higher ✓

    • High 6: $629.47 → High 7: $630.00 ↑ higher ✓

    • High 8: $636.60 → High 9: $629.98 ↓ lower ✗
    • High 9: $629.98 → High 10: $617.52 ↓ lower ✗

    • High 11: $606.48 → High 12: $609.78 ↑ higher ✓
    • High 12: $609.78 → High 13: $610.35 ↑ higher ✓
    • High 13: $610.35 → High 14: $616.83 ↑ higher ✓

  • Higher Lows (HL) check:
    • Low 1: $600.28 → Low 2: $617.78 ↑ higher ✓
    • Low 2: $617.78 → Low 3: $618.73 ↑ higher ✓
    • Low 3: $618.73 → Low 4: $610.15 ↓ lower ✗
    • Low 4: $610.15 → Low 5: $617.80 ↑ higher ✓
    • Low 5: $617.80 → Low 6: $614.56 ↓ lower ✗
    • Low 6: $614.56 → Low 7: $607.05 ↓ lower ✗

    • Low 18: $602.55 → Low 19: $608.11 ↑ higher ✓

Criterion 2: Linear Regression Fit (R²)

  • R² of primary-side pivots: 0.947 (threshold: ≥ 0.5)
  • R² measures how well the pivots cluster around a straight line. A low R² means the swings are erratic — the "trend" would just be connecting random noise.

Criterion 3: Slope Significance

  • Primary trendline slope: 0.1555% per bar
  • Slope check: PASS — 0.1555% is within [0.007%, 0.30%]
  • The minimum slope (0.005%/bar) prevents a nearly horizontal line from being declared a trend. A flat channel is SIDEWAYS, not a trend.

Criterion 4: Volume Confirmation

  • Volume verdict: DIVERGENT — with-trend volume is only 0.86× counter-trend (threshold ≥ 1.1×). Volume is not expanding on the dominant legs.
  • Confidence reduced by 15% (from 0.96 → 0.81).
  • Volume is non-blocking — it adjusts confidence but does not on its own flip the regime to OTHERS. This follows Pring's framework: volume divergence is a warning, not an immediate regime change signal.

Final Verdict

  • Regime: TREND (UPTREND)
  • Confidence: 0.81 (1.0 = fully confirmed, ≥0.70 = high conviction)
  • R²: 0.947
  • Trend starts at bar: 542

4. Step 3: Trendline Channel

The channel is built using a two-pass approach:

  1. Pass 1a (primary line): Linear regression through the primary-side pivots (highs for uptrend, lows for downtrend), then the line is shifted so it passes through — but does not cut into — the most extreme pivot.
  2. Pass 1b (opposite line): A parallel line at the same slope, anchored to the most extreme opposite-side pivot (low for uptrend, high for downtrend).
  3. Pass 2 (geometry validation): The residual scatter between the two lines is examined to classify whether the channel is parallel, wedging, broadening, or triangular.

Primary Trendline

  • Role: SUPPORT
  • Direction: Rising — slope = 0.9517 price units per bar
  • R² of regression fit: 0.938 (how tightly the pivots cluster around the line)
  • Construction method: REGRESSION
  • Steep flag: No
  • Anchor points used:
    • Bar 542 (03/30 19:00): $555.60
    • Bar 557 (04/02 13:30): $571.92
    • Bar 573 (04/07 15:00): $578.40
    • Bar 584 (04/08 19:00): $602.55
    • Bar 599 (04/13 13:30): $608.11

The chart below shows the primary trendline only, without the opposite line. This lets you see which pivots anchor the primary fit and how closely they align.

Opposite (Parallel/Bounding) Line

  • Anchor point:
    • Bar 554 (04/01 17:00): $587.74
    • Bar 565 (04/06 14:00): $590.61
    • Bar 594 (04/10 15:00): $613.67
  • The opposite line is placed at the same slope as the primary line and shifted to just touch — without being exceeded by — the most extreme opposite-side pivot. This defines the far boundary of the channel.

Channel Geometry

  • Detected geometry: RISING_WEDGE
  • Both lines slope upward but converge — support is rising faster than resistance. This is a bearish pattern: the rally is narrowing, suggesting buyers are running out of room. Resolution is typically downward.
  • Resolution bias: BEARISH

Channel Width

  • Width: 1.7% of midpoint price
  • Width in ATR units: 3.0× ATR(14)
  • Valid range: 1.5% – 20.0% (percentage check) | 2.0× – 8.0× ATR (volatility cross-check)
  • Status: VALID
  • A channel that is too narrow (< 1.5%) is likely fitting noise rather than meaningful structure. One that is too wide (> 20.0%) may be catching multiple distinct trend phases.
  • Current price position: UPPER_QUARTER — 239% of the way from the lower line to the upper line (0% = at lower boundary, 100% = at upper boundary). Current price: $640.41.

The chart below adds the opposite line, completing the full channel.

5. Step 4: Support & Resistance Zones

S/R zones are horizontal price bands where the market has repeatedly reversed. The engine clusters nearby pivot prices and scores each cluster on four dimensions:

Scoring Formula

  • Touch count × 2.0 — each time price tests the zone from above (resistance) or below (support) without decisively breaking through, the zone gains 2.0 points.
  • Recency × 1.5 — more recent touches score higher. Zones not retested within 200 bars have their score halved (time decay, per Bulkowski).
  • Volume at touches × 1.0 — high-volume reversals at the zone confirm it is a meaningful level (not thin-market noise).
  • Role reversal bonus × 3.0 — if the level previously acted as support and now acts as resistance (or vice versa), it scores an extra 3.0 points. Former support becomes resistance after a breakdown, and vice versa — a well-established concept in technical analysis.

Acceptance Criteria

  • Minimum score: ≥ 4.0
  • Minimum touches: ≥ 2
  • Zone clustering tolerance: 0.4% of price (or 0.5× ATR, whichever is larger) — pivots within this band are grouped into one zone.
  • Zones weaken by 10% per touch beyond the 3rd (0.90^(touches-2) multiplier) — repeated tests erode the zone's resistance to future breakouts.

Active S/R Zones (sorted by score)

  • Zone age: 131 bars since formation
  • Score breakdown:
    • Touch component: 4 × 2.0 = 8.0
    • Role reversal bonus: 1 × 3.0 = 3.0.0
    • Recency + volume components: ≈ 2.79 (exact split not stored — depends on how recently the touches occurred and volume at each touch)

6. Step 5: Volume Analysis

Volume is examined at three levels: trend-wide, pivot-by-pivot, and on-balance flow. None of these are binary pass/fail — they adjust the confidence in the regime classification.

Trend Volume Ratio

The engine computes average volume on bars moving in the trend direction versus bars moving against it. A healthy trend should attract more volume on its dominant legs.

  • With-trend / counter-trend volume ratio: 0.86× (threshold for confirmation: ≥ 1.1×)
  • Volume verdict: DIVERGENT
  • Volume expanding AGAINST trend (ratio=0.86) — bearish divergence

Pivot-Level Volume Divergence

Volume divergence occurs when successive pivots in the trend direction show decreasing volume — price is still making new highs/lows but fewer participants are driving those moves. This is a classic warning from Pring.

  • Divergence warning: SIGNIFICANT (2 consecutive divergent pivot pair(s))
  • The "VD" red flags on the chart mark the specific pivots where volume was lower than the prior same-side pivot.
  • Pivot $590.61: volume ratio 0.59×, prior pivot vol 7945832 — divergent ⚠
  • Pivot $613.67: volume ratio 0.78×, prior pivot vol 4764733 — divergent ⚠

On-Balance Volume (OBV)

OBV accumulates volume on up-bars and subtracts it on down-bars. Its slope direction tells us whether money is generally flowing into or out of the stock over the trend period.

  • OBV slope direction: POSITIVE (slope = 1335170.3942 per bar, R² = 0.826)
  • OBV vs price trend: CONFIRMED — OBV direction matches the price trend direction (healthy confirmation).
  • The purple OBV line and its dashed trendline appear in the lower subplot of the final chart.

8. Fan Principle

When the primary trendline breaks but the broader trend structure is still intact, the engine redraws trendlines at shallower slopes using only the post-break pivots. This is the fan principle — each successive redraw "fans out" at a decreasing angle.

  • Maximum fans before exhaustion signal: 3
  • When the third fan line breaks, the engine sets fan_exhausted = True and signals a likely trend reversal.

Fan Lines

  • Fan line 1 (slope: 0.9517 price/bar)
    • Anchor points:
      • Bar 542 (03/30 19:00): $555.60
      • Bar 557 (04/02 13:30): $571.92
      • Bar 573 (04/07 15:00): $578.40
      • Bar 584 (04/08 19:00): $602.55
      • Bar 599 (04/13 13:30): $608.11
    • Each successive fan is flatter than the previous one — the trend is decelerating, retesting at increasingly shallow angles.

9. Final Verdict & Key Takeaways

The 1-hour chart for this period is classified as TREND, direction: UPTREND with confidence 0.81. Price is moving within a RISING_WEDGE channel (width 1.7%), currently in the UPPER_QUARTER (239% through channel). Volume is diverging — the trend is moving on declining participation.

Key Takeaways

  • Regime: TREND, direction: UPTREND — confidence 0.81
  • R²: 0.947
  • Channel: RISING_WEDGE, width 1.7%, status VALID
  • Current position: UPPER_QUARTER (239% through channel)
  • Volume: Divergent ⚠
  • Nearest support: $627.76

What to Watch

  • A close below the lower channel boundary (~$633.81) with elevated volume would suggest the uptrend is breaking down.
